topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The hammering started at seven this morning, a relentless, jarring assault on my peace. I watched from behind my blinds, my jaw tightening until my molars ached. Every rhythmic thud seemed to vibrate through the floorboards, a deliberate intrusion. When the neighbor, Mr. Henderson, finally appeared, wiping sweat from his brow with a greasy rag, his smile was wide and entirely too cheerful. "Just getting this done, neighbor!" he called out, his voice carrying over the sound of the construction. I stood up, my hands clenching into fists so tight my knuckles turned white. "Mr. Henderson," I began, my voice dangerously level, "that fence needs to be completely out of sight of my property line. This placement is unacceptable." He just chuckled, unperturbed, already inspecting a post. The sheer audacity of it made my stomach clench with a hot, acidic feeling.

[story 2]

I paced the length of my backyard five times before I finally confronted him. My breath came in short, sharp bursts, and I kept running a hand over the back of my neck, trying to steady the tremor starting in my hands. "Look," I managed, trying to keep the tremor out of my tone, "I understand you want a boundary, but this is encroaching. It’s right on the edge of my garden. I specifically asked that this installation be done further back." He leaned against the newly erected, half-finished wooden slats, crossing his arms with an air of complete nonchalance. "Oh, come on, Bob. It’s perfectly fine. It looks great. We’ll just adjust the footing later." The casual dismissal of my request felt like a physical slap. I stared at the fence, my vision narrowing to a single, angry point.

[story 3]

The sight of the lumber piled haphazardly near my property line sent a spike of pure aggravation through me. I walked over, not bothering with pleasantries, and pointed a rigid finger at the lowest section of the proposed barrier. "This is where it stops," I stated, my voice tight, every syllable edged with strain. I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a flush of sheer frustration. The neighbor looked up, his expression shifting from relaxed to mildly surprised, as if I were suddenly a wild animal. "Why the fuss, Mark?" he asked, sounding genuinely confused. "It’s just a fence. It serves a purpose." I shook my head slowly, a deep, frustrated sigh escaping my lips. "It serves *your* purpose, perhaps. Mine is keeping my view clear, and this is entirely disregarding that."
